1: The narrative's temporal marker: ûdəḇar YHWH hāyāh yāqār bayyāmîm hahēm ʾên ḥāzôn niphrāṣ — 'the word of YHWH was rare in those days; there was no frequent vision.' The rarity of prophetic vision at the end of the Eli period marks a nadir of covenant communication — YHWH had gone silent. This silence is the negative background against which Samuel's emergence as prophet is the more dramatic. The pattern of divine silence followed by renewed prophetic word is a recurrent OT structure: the centuries between Malachi and John the Baptist, the silence between Elijah and Elisha's ministry. Amos 8:11-12 describes a coming 'famine of hearing the words of YHWH' — the absence of prophetic word as divine judgment. The silence at Shiloh is not YHWH's absence but his withholding of the word that Eli's priesthood has made itself unworthy to receive.
3: Samuel sleeps 'in the temple of YHWH where the ark of God was.' The physical arrangement of the Shiloh sanctuary described here — the Ark, the lamp of God, the sleeping quarters for the priestly attendant — places Samuel in the position of the ideal sanctuary-keeper: present, attentive, in the posture of availability. The lamp of God (nēr ʾelōhîm) burning through the night is the mənōrāh's lamp (Exodus 27:20-21 — the priests are to keep the lamp burning from evening to morning). Samuel's nighttime vigil at the Ark, hearing the divine voice while Eli's sight is failing (v2 — 'his eyes had begun to grow dim'), is a visual narrative of the transition: the old, dimming priestly order yields to the attentive young servant who hears what Eli can no longer see or receive.
19: Samuel's prophetic authentication: wayyiḡdal šᵉmûʾēl wᵉYHWH hāyāh ʿimmô wəlōʾ hippîl mikkol dᵉḇārāyw ʾārṣāh — 'Samuel grew and YHWH was with him and did not let any of his words fall to the ground.' The phrase 'words falling to the ground' (hippîl dāḇār ʾārṣāh) is the idiom for prophetic failure — a word that does not come to pass. The canonical criterion for true prophecy in Deuteronomy 18:22 is precisely this: if the word does not come to pass, the prophet has spoken presumptuously. Samuel's authentication is the inverse: all his words come true. This is the foundational credential that makes Samuel the paradigmatic OT prophet and the institutional anchor for what will be called the 'school of the prophets' (bᵉnê hannəḇîʾîm) in the Elijah-Elisha narratives (2 Kgs 2-4). Second Temple Judaism's high regard for Samuel is reflected in Sir 1 Samuel 46:13-20 and 1 Macc 1 Samuel 2:57 — his intercession at Mizpah was remembered as paradigmatic covenant prayer.
2: Eli, whose eyesight had grown so dim that he could no longer see, was lying down in his room. The detail of Eli’s failing eyesight is both physically accurate for advanced age and symbolically loaded: the priest responsible for spiritual discernment cannot see clearly. The narrative has already shown his failure to discern Hannah’s prayer and his failure to discipline his sons; now his physical blindness externalizes his spiritual state. Vision failures accompanying moral/discernment failures appear with Isaac (Genesis 27:1) and form a recurring prophetic metaphor (Isaiah 6:9-10; John 9:39).
4: YHWH called, “Samuel!” and the boy answered, “Here I am (hinneni).” The response hinneni is the classic answer of readiness to divine call: Abraham at the Aqedah (Genesis 22:1, 7, 11), Jacob at Bethel (Genesis 46:2), Moses at the burning bush (Exodus 3:4), Isaiah at the throne vision (Isaiah 6:8). It is the readiness-response of the servant before the sovereign. Samuel’s immediate hinneni signals his essential posture even before he understands who is calling.
5: Samuel ran to Eli: “Here I am, for you called me.” Eli’s denial: “I did not call you; lie down again (shub shkav).” The threefold structure of call-and-return (1 Samuel 3:5, 6, 8) is a standard narrative device emphasizing the persistence and clarity of a divine call over against human confusion. The triple occurrence rules out coincidence or misperception. The three-stage initiation into prophetic calling mirrors the call-narrative patterns of the major prophets, where the prophet’s initial resistance or confusion is overcome by divine persistence.
6: The second call: YHWH called again, and again Samuel ran to Eli saying “Here I am, for you called me.” Eli’s denial: “I did not call, my son (beni); lie down again.” Eli’s use of beni (my son) is tender — he regards Samuel as a ward. The narrative irony is sharp: the child who is not his son is more truly a son than those who are. Psalms 2:7 (“You are my Son; today I have begotten you”) and Hebrews 1:5 use the father-son language for the divine call of the ultimate Son; the Samuel narrative is a type of divine adoption-through-call.
7: “Now Samuel did not yet know the LORD (lo yada’ et-YHWH), and the word of the LORD had not yet been revealed to him (terem yiggaleh elav).” Samuel’s failure to recognize the divine call is not spiritual dullness but inexperience. He has grown up in the sanctuary, serving YHWH faithfully, but has not yet had a personal divine encounter. The prophetic word comes to the human ear before it is recognized as such — a distinction between information and encounter that marks genuine prophetic initiation.
8: The third call and the third trip to Eli; then “Eli understood that the LORD was calling the boy (vayavin Eli ki YHWH qore’ la-na’ar).” Eli’s recognition is the turning point. The aged priest who has failed spiritually in so many ways performs here an act of genuine service: he recognizes divine activity and channels it toward the child. There is pathos in the spiritual director whose own house is condemned serving as the guide for the one who will replace him. Eli’s role at this moment is analogous to John the Baptist’s: pointing beyond himself to the one who comes after (John 1:27).
9: Eli’s instruction: “If he calls you, you shall say, ‘Speak, LORD, for your servant (ki shome’a avdekha) is listening.’” The formula of the receptive servant reverses the common human posture of speaking and expecting to be heard. Isaiah’s “Here I am; send me” (Isaiah 6:8) shares the structure of readiness for divine communication. Mary’s “I am the Lord’s servant; let it be to me according to your word” (Luke 1:38) is the New Covenant version of Samuel’s posture — the receptive servant opened to transformative divine address.
10: YHWH came and stood (vayavo YHWH vayityatzev), calling as before: “Samuel! Samuel!” — the double name-call that marks moments of special divine address throughout the OT (Abraham: Genesis 22:11; Jacob: Genesis 46:2; Moses: Exodus 3:4). The doubled name signals urgency and the gravity of what follows. Samuel answered with the receptive formula Eli taught him; YHWH proceeds to reveal his word.
11: “Behold, I am about to do something in Israel that will make the ears of everyone who hears it tingle (tetzallenah shetei oznahv).” The tingling ears formula signals an oracle of overwhelming significance. It appears again in 2 Kgs 21:12 and Jeremiah 19:3, both pronouncements of catastrophic divine action. YHWH’s first direct word to Samuel is not an encouraging commission but a judgment oracle against Eli’s house. The prophetic vocation begins not with comfort but with truth about divine judgment.
12: “On that day I will carry out against Eli everything I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end (heichel ve’killah).” The finality of the oracle — from beginning to end, nothing omitted — emphasizes totality. The contrast with divine mercy (which endures forever, Ps 136) is striking: judgment is also total when it comes, but it comes only after sustained patience and warning.
13: “His sons were blaspheming God (meqallelim lahem) and he did not rebuke them (lo kihah bahem).” The verb qalal (curse/treat with contempt) is used here for what Eli’s sons did to God. The indictment of Eli is precisely: he knew (yada’) and did not restrain (kihah). Knowledge plus inaction equals complicity. James 3:1 (teachers judged more strictly) and Hebrews 13:17 (leaders give an account for those in their care) apply the same standard to NT leadership.
14: “The iniquity of Eli’s house shall never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering (im-yithkhapper avon beit eli bizevach uminchah ad olam).” One of the most sobering statements in the OT: a sin beyond sacrifice-and-offering atonement. The Levitical system provided for most sins, but Eli’s house has crossed into the territory of the yad ramah (high-handed sin) of Numbers 15:30-31 — deliberate defiance for which no atonement is prescribed. Hebrews 10:26-27 uses similar logic for willful apostasy after receiving the knowledge of the truth.
15: Samuel lay until morning, then opened the doors of the house of YHWH. He was afraid to tell Eli the vision (the mar’eh). The narrative notes that Samuel opens the sanctuary doors — his practical morning duty that frames the extraordinary event of the night. The ordinary and the extraordinary are woven together: even after receiving a devastating prophetic revelation, life continues in its quotidian rhythms. Prophets are not exempted from ordinary obligations by revelatory experience.
16: Eli called Samuel: “Samuel, my son (beni).” Samuel replied “Here I am (hinneni)” — the same word he used in response to the divine call. The word now carries double resonance: he is present and ready both for God and for Eli. Eli’s request that follows — “Do not hide it from me” — shows more courage than his past behavior; he chooses to hear the truth rather than avoid it. Job’s demand to hear from God even when the answer was devastating (Job 13:3, 22) shares this posture of demanding difficult truth.
17: Eli’s charge: “What did he say to you? Do not hide it from me. May God deal with you severely if you hide anything from me.” The standard Hebrew oath formula invokes divine punishment for concealment. Eli uses the language of divine sovereignty he has failed to apply to his sons — he invokes YHWH’s judgment on potential concealment even while having tolerated his sons’ blasphemy. But the request is also courageous: Eli is demanding the full truth about his own condemnation.
18: Samuel told everything and hid nothing. Eli’s response: “He is the LORD (YHWH hu). Let him do what seems right in his eyes (ha-tov be-einav ya’aseh).” Whatever Eli’s spiritual state, the words are theologically correct: YHWH’s assessment of what is right governs outcomes, not human preference. The acceptance of divine judgment — even against oneself — is a spiritual posture Job models (Job 1:21: “the LORD gave and the LORD has taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD”).
20: “All Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that Samuel had been established as a prophet of the LORD (ne’eman Samuel le-navi laYHWH).” The formula “from Dan to Beersheba” is the standard north-to-south expression for the entire land (Judges 20:1; 2 Samuel 3:10). The verb ne’eman (confirmed/established) is related to amen — his prophetic status is ratified by fulfilled prophecy. Deuteronomy 18:22’s criterion for a true prophet (his words come true) is the implicit standard being applied here.
21: “The LORD continued to appear (vayyoseph YHWH lehera’ot) at Shiloh, for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel at Shiloh through the word of the LORD.” The repeated divine appearance through the medium of Samuel marks the beginning of a new era of revelation. The phrase “through the word of the LORD (bidfar YHWH)” identifies the prophetic word as the primary vehicle of divine self-disclosure. John 1:1-3 elevates this to its maximum expression: the Word (Logos) is not merely a vehicle of revelation but divine revelation in person. Samuel is the vessel through whom the word comes; Jesus is the Word.
